seo_title: The Sensation Modulating Network: Haltability as Architectural…
meta_description: A new cognitive agent architecture, the Sensation Modulating Network (SMN), proposes haltability as a key feature for object-directed phenomenology.
body_html:
The Sensation Modulating Network (SMN) proposes haltability as a key feature for object-directed phenomenology. This architectural ground enables co-activated equilibrium, which is essential for intentional directedness and grammar-grounded generativity.

The SMN conceives the cognitive agent as the whole body, organized at every anatomical scale by opponent dynamics. The Sensation Modulators sense and act through one substrate, paired into Coordinated Action Zones routed by a body-wide broadcast network.

Haltability: the recruitment of antagonistic affordance into co-activated equilibrium provides the architectural locus for object-directed phenomenology. Dual-signal property of self-modulatable action patterns (SMAPs) makes the self/world distinction a structural feature of the wiring rather than a category the agent applies.

Predicted Registers and Formalism Eight predicted registers, with reference simulations, are given in an appendix. A tentative formalism is also proposed, reconciling the cognitivism-4E debate.

source.title: The Sensation Modulating Network:Haltability as the architectural ground for object-directed phenomenology
source.summary:
Here is a summary of the news item in 2-4 sentences:

Researchers propose a new cognitive architecture called the Sensation Modulating Network (SMN) to reconcile the differences between cognitivism and 4E approaches in cognitive science. The SMN posits that cognition arises from the whole body, organized by opponent dynamics and paired into coordinated action zones, allowing for object-directed phenomenology. This architecture provides a framework for understanding recursion, embodiment, and generativity, and offers a potential solution to the long-standing debate between cognitivism and 4E approaches. The SMN also predicts specific features of cognition, including eight registers that can be tested through simulations.
